nativeRange.habitat: Coastal reefs with rubble, rock, and sand bottoms at depths of approximately 12–35 m; typically encountered singly or in pairs.

decorPreferences: deep fine-grain sand bed (5–10 cm) for burrowing and sifting
stable live rock arranged on tank bottom (not atop sand)
sparse open sand areas for foraging

diet.notes: Feeds naturally by sifting sand and filtering small invertebrates, particularly copepods and amphipods. In captivity generally accepts frozen mysis shrimp, enriched brine shrimp, and small marine pellets; a mature refugium producing live copepods is commonly recommended to supplement prepared foods. Feeding frequency of two to three times daily tends to work well for this active sifter.

avoidWith: aggressive or fast-moving fish that may stress them
sand-dwelling corals and sea stars that may be buried by sifting
blennies (have been known to trigger territorial disputes)

summary: A small, colorful sand-sifting sleeper goby from the Western Pacific, noted for its sapphire-blue cheek stripes and yellow dorsal filaments; generally peaceful and considered reef-safe with caution regarding sand-bed invertebrates.
careNotes:
Valenciennea bella is one of the smaller Valenciennea species — FishBase records a maximum standard length of 6.9 cm (~2.7 in) and Quality Marine notes it rarely exceeds 3 inches (~7.6 cm), while LiveAquaria's product page lists 6 inches (15.2 cm) which is likely a data entry error possibly carried over from V. puellaris (Diamond Goby); the smaller figures from FishBase and specialist sources are used here. Temperature guidance similarly diverged: LiveAquaria recommends 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C) while FishBase habitat modeling indicates 27.1–29.1°C; a cross-source averaged band of 24–28°C is used. Both issues are flagged for reviewer confirmation.

This species sifts sand almost continuously, filtering small invertebrates through its gills. This behavior usefully aerates the substrate but may bury sand-dwelling corals or displace sand-bed organisms; placing corals on rockwork rather than in the sand bed tends to work better with this goby. Aquarists commonly suggest a tank footprint emphasising length over height to provide adequate sifting territory.

V. bella has been documented forming monogamous pairs in the wild and tends to be kept as a bonded pair in the aquarium; splitting established pairs is generally not advised. Males develop a distinctive black throat and elongated second-to-fourth dorsal fin filaments. The species is a well-known jumper — a tight-fitting lid is considered essential. It has been observed becoming territorial toward other gobies, blennies, or dartfish, particularly in smaller aquariums.

A populated refugium producing copepods and amphipods is widely regarded as beneficial for long-term health and conditioning. Also sold as 'Yellow Filament Goby' in reference to the male's extended dorsal filaments.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: The gobies collection page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/gobies) listed the product but did not include species details; the canonical product page was accessed directly. Note: LiveAquaria's stated maximum size of 6 inches (15.2 cm) appears to be an outlier inconsistent with FishBase and specialist sources — see careNotes.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Hoese & Larson, 1994), taxonomy (Gobiidae / Gobiiformes), maximum length (6.9 cm SL), temperature range (27.1–29.1°C habitat model), native range (Okinawa Japan and Philippines), depth range (12–35 m), diet (small invertebrates, particularly copepods), and behavioral notes (solitary or in pairs).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Specialist trade article confirming small size ('rarely exceeds three inches'), monogamous pair-bonding, jumping behavior, sand-bed requirements (grain size <2mm, 2.5–5 cm depth), tank footprint emphasis, feeding frequency (minimum 3x daily), and reef-safety caveats regarding sand-dwelling corals. Tank minimum stated as 40-gallon breeder (~151 L) for a mated pair — used in tank-size cross-verification.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (6.9 cm / ~2.7 in confirming FishBase), temperature range (27.1–29.1°C), minimum tank recommendation (300 L / ~79 gal), and additional common names (Yellow Filament Goby, Bella Grundel). Native range expanded to include Indonesia, Raja Ampat, Ryukyu Islands, and Bangai Archipelago.
